title: Quadlet: Container with `Notify=healthy` does not respect `HealthStartPeriod` larger than systemd `TimeoutStartSec`
body:
### Issue Description

When starting a container via quadlet with the setting `Notify=healthy`, values for `HealthStartPeriod` larger than the default systemd `TimeoutStartSec` (default: 90s) are not respected.  
This is because with `Notify=healthy` systemd will see the service as starting until the startup heathcheck succeeds. So after it sees the service starting for longer than its own startup timeout it will stop the service and mark it as failed (systemd logs `systemd[1]: healthcheck.service: start operation timed out. Terminating.` and `systemd[1]: healthcheck.service: Failed with result 'timeout'.`).

This issue can be fixed by setting `TimeoutStartSec` on the service. Either to `infinity` to disable the timeout in systemd or to `HealthStartPeriod` plus some extra time to account for image pulls, container creation, etc.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

- Add this quadlet file to `/etc/containers/systemd`:
```
[Container]
Image=docker.io/library/alpine:latest
Exec=/bin/sh -c "sleep 180; touch /startup_done; sleep 999999"

Notify=healthy
HealthStartupRetries=0
HealthStartPeriod=20m

HealthStartupCmd=["/usr/bin/test", "-f", "/startup_done"]
HealthStartupInterval=10s
HealthStartupTimeout=2s
HealthCmd=["/usr/bin/test", "-f", "/startup_done"]
HealthInterval=1m
HealthTimeout=2s

# Uncomment this to fix the issue
# [Service]
# TimeoutStartSec=infinity
```
- `sudo systemctl daemon-reload`
- `sudo systemctl start healthcheck.service`
- Wait for it to fail
- Optional: Check `/run/systemd/generator/healthcheck.service` to see no generated `TimeoutStartSec` setting
- Uncomment the `[Service]` section in the quadlet file
- `sudo systemctl daemon-reload`
- `sudo systemctl start healthcheck.service`
- Wait for it to start successfully

### Describe the results you received

The service fails to start

```
# systemctl status healthcheck.service
× healthcheck.service
     Loaded: loaded (/etc/containers/systemd/healthcheck.container; generated)
     Active: failed (Result: timeout) since Tue 2025-10-14 12:30:29 CEST; 30s ago
```

### Describe the results you expected

The service starts without problem

```
# systemctl status healthcheck.service
● healthcheck.service
     Loaded: loaded (/etc/containers/systemd/healthcheck.container; generated)
     Active: active (running) since Tue 2025-10-14 12:35:12 CEST; 6s ago
```

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

Privileged

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

Tested an Archlinux using distro podman (see info output above) reproduced on a Debian Trixie VM using git version 32d6c540542df8bf2115a8600fd4fbbf4b6b837a  

### Additional information

Additional information like issue happens only occasionally or issue happens with a particular architecture or on a particular setting

comments.body: @ygalblum PTAL. It seems like it could be reasonable to error on health startup period longer than TimeoutStartSeconds?

> It seems like it could be reasonable to error on health startup period longer than TimeoutStartSeconds?

Personal note: When I set `HealthStartPeriod` I have already expressed the desire to allow the container a certain time to start up. When that did not work, I kept looking for other quadlet options that I may need to set to allow the container to start fully (example: `HealthStartupRetries` is also needed). I would have expected quadlet to set the systemd `TimeoutStartSeconds` option.

Technical note: The default value for `TimeoutStartSeconds` is configurable via `DefaultTimeoutStartSec` in `systemd-system.conf` which has various locations for config and drop-ins. Determining the correct value to compare to may be complicated.

> Technical note: The default value for TimeoutStartSeconds is configurable via DefaultTimeoutStartSec in systemd-system.conf which has various locations for config and drop-ins. Determining the correct value to compare to may be complicated.

correct that is impossible more or less in the scope of quadlet

What we did for image pull is to use EXTEND_TIMEOUT_USEC on the notify socket which is much nicer then overwriting TimeoutStartSeconds=

Because that way we can update it dynamically only if we actually wait for the healthcheck, if there is a bug and we are let's say deadlocked in a different code part it doesn't have to wait so long and can use the shorter timeout value. So I suggest we do the same here.

https://github.com/containers/container-libs/blob/a3b0f19c3ea12cb02f0b11788489c52d4fb02e96/common/libimage/copier.go#L392-L421

I did not know about EXTEND_TIMEOUT_USEC, that does sound like a much better solution.

With a quick search I found the [waitForHealthy](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/86eecc976a7e38a0759dcbd937912af46d0ee9ed/libpod/container_internal.go#L1335) function, which waits for the container to be healthy and then sends the final READY=1 message. That or the [WaitForConditionWithInterval](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/86eecc976a7e38a0759dcbd937912af46d0ee9ed/libpod/container_api.go#L711) function it calls look like good candidates for adding that functionality to me.
@Luap99 so, IIUC your suggestion is a fix inside Podman, not in Quadlet. Right?

> [@Luap99](https://github.com/Luap99) so, IIUC your suggestion is a fix inside Podman, not in Quadlet. Right?

correct that would go into the podman code path

So far I understand: with `Notify=healthy`, the larger value of `HealthStartPeriod` getting ineffective because startup healthcheck is taking more than default time (`TimeoutStartSec `). But instead of dynamically overwrite `TimeoutStartSec`, we can use   `EXTEND_TIMEOUT_USEC` in [waitForHealthy](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/86eecc976a7e38a0759dcbd937912af46d0ee9ed/libpod/container_internal.go#L1335) or [WaitForConditionWithInterval](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/86eecc976a7e38a0759dcbd937912af46d0ee9ed/libpod/container_api.go#L711). Am I correct?? or missing something?
